comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"The good occult student should have a sound general knowledge of natural science, history, mathematics, and philosophy. He cannot, naturally, have a thorough knowledge of all these subjects, but he should know their outlines; he should be familiar with the principles of all the sciences and know the methods of philosophy.  Then, when he acquires special knowledge, he will be able to see it in relation to the cosmic scheme of which it forms a part, and hence will know it in a very different way from the man who perceives it apart from its environment." 
 

That's from Dion Fortune's The Training and Work of the Initiate, one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will probably be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed.  See you next week!*** 

[IMAGE:1]


And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

comments.title: Question, info and acronym list
comments.content:
Hello and thank you for hosting Magic Monday!

Question

I'm putting together the first four gods and goddesses to start the SOP ritual, and I think recently Ceridwen was in contact with me in a mild and encouraging way.

I feel drawn to the Celtic pantheon, and was looking at online lists after copying out the instructions from Magic Monday 12/1/19's comment thread.

Several seem appropriate, especially those who guide and protect artisans, poets, woodlands, waters, and crossroads (such as Brigit, Druantia, Flidaish, and Lugh, Merlin, Nuada and Cernunnos).

I wondered how to tell which are elder and which are younger, to meet the guidelines from the MM instructions about invoking the elder of the two gods first, then the younger god, then the elder goddess and then the younger goddess.

If you have a suggestion for the four to begin with, I would be very interested in your guidance.

Info

(this may be more appropriate for the open thread at the other site, please delete if so and I'll repost it there)

Mercury in retrograde was hard to get through...I'm a writer but got very little written. But I did manage to finish the second part of a series about the potential for Centre County Pennsylvania's people to grow much of the food to support the county population, based on a 2001 master's thesis by a Penn State student.

In response, one of my readers contacted me with a caution that the populations of the megacities on the East Coast may well follow I-80 on foot to get to the farm areas of the Appalachians and Alleghenies, under hunger pressures.

This reader also asked if I'd looked into the potential for regional populations to produce our own machines. He provided a couple of links to me, that I wanted to pass along to you, about "Open Source Ecology"

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Source_Ecology

and "Global Village Construction Set."

https://www.opensourceecology.org/gvcs/

On first look, the organization and project seem heavy on computer coding for the 3-D printer as a panacea, which is not quite what I think of as appropriate tech, since it relies on the computer-based information distribution and electrical network.

But it's an interesting piece of the puzzle, just knowing that the organization exists and its membership is working on these things.

Feeling better since retrograde passed, have started an affirmation practice, and started typesetting a first lengthier project (text longer than an address block or short quotation) - the list of foods that can be grown in Centre County according to the 2001 thesis.

Starter Acronym translator

As suggested last week, here's a first go at providing a list of acronyms that pop up a lot here and at Ecosophia, and the meanings as I understand them so far. I'll probably be able to add another batch in a couple weeks after going through some more archives. ---KW

ADR - Archdruid Report (JMG's former blog)
AFAIK - "As far as I know" (idiom)
AODA - Ancient Order of Druids in America (organization)
APG - Art and Practice of Geomancy (book by JMG)
CD - Cosmic Doctrine (book by Dion Fortune)
CGD - Celtic Golden Dawn. (book by JMG)
DA - Dolmen Arch (book by JMG)
DH - The Druidry Handbook (book by JMG)
DMH - The Druid Magic Handbook (book by JMG)
GCC - Gnostic Celtic Church (book by JMG)
GD - Golden Dawn or Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn (organization; occult system)
LIRP - Lesser Invoking Ritual of the Pentagram (ritual)
LBRP - Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram (ritual)
LM - Lucky Mojo - (occult store - online and brick-and-mortar magic store in California)
LRM - Learning Ritual Magic (book by JMG and others).
MM - Magic Monday (weekly JMG instructional thread at Dreamwidth)
MP or MPE - Middle Pillar Exercise (ritual exercise)
OBE - out of body experience
OBOD - Order of Bards, Ovates and Druids (organization)
OP - original poster (person who started the topical thread at JMG's Dreamwidth)
PMC - professional managerial class (idiom to describe a political/socioeconomic class)
SOP - Sphere of Protection (ritual)
SJW - social justice warrior (idiom to describe a political viewpoint)
TDS - Trump Derangement Syndrome (idiom to describe a political viewpoint)
TLH - ?
TSW - "This stuff works" (idiom)
YMMV - "Your mileage may vary" (idiom)

comments.title: Moon hex
comments.content:
Hey JMG, since someone is going to mention it, it might as well be me… on Sunday an extremely amusing Twitter thread took over the entire site for most of the day, permanently infiltrating people’s minds with the question of “hexing the moon.”

As this link describes, no one has publicly taken credit for the hex, it’s just a rumor that was repeated by leftist witches on TikTok. One of the rumormongers claims that the hex on the moon was meant to support Trump, while another says that “baby witches” are just doing it for attention; not a lot to it. However, the rumor really took off when it was framed on Twitter in the terms of a teenage call-out thread (you know the type, they open with “ok it’s time to talk about the _____”), which produced an uncanny sensation for the non-witches reading it.

The thread now has almost 30,000 retweets, which probably includes a lot of outsiders. However, many of the ensuing discussions among leftists take witchcraft surprisingly seriously, with the old militant atheists of the mid-2000s Internet being distinctly in the minority.

You’ve commented before on the misuse of hexes by the leftist witches but I see a bit of a development in this thread, namely, a widespread awareness that it’s not a good idea to throw hexes on random things you dislike. I also recently bumped into the story of a Facebook witchery group that imploded after the admins naively endorsed grave robbery as part of its no-rules approach, with legitimately initiated Yoruba practitioners leading the group’s permanent dissolution.

Perhaps witches are learning from their missteps? Are things moving in a positive direction?

Hello JMG,

some questions for today:

1) I heard an old interview with a "sorcerer" who seems to practice a mix of ceremonial magic (goetic end of it, it seems) and folk magic.
He told the interviewer about an occasion where he invoked/summoned one of the bigger demons. During this occasion he seems to have found out that his LBRP was of no use as a barrier to the demon, who could pass right through it.

I remember you saying that summoning demons is easy when you are already on their level. Could it be that a banishing ritual will do you no good when you are already jumping into the pit by your own will, metaphorically speaking? How about people who don't want to have any relations with demons and keep a regular banishing practice - can demons break through this barrier?

2) Let's say I have a prehistorical site in my neighborhood and want to know more about it. Could I do a DMH grove ceremony and call the spirit of place, so that he may tell me something about the history of the site? Any precautions I should take when I try this?

Thank you for magic monday!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The LBRP is a good protective ritual, and it's quite adequate to keep noxious spirits at bay -- so long as you don't do something idiotic like performing a ritual to summon a demon into manifestation. If you do that, you need much heavier artillery. You can avoid that kind of trouble by not summoning demons!

2) That could be exceedingly risky, if the site was used for human sacrifice or other malefic workings -- and that did happen, you know. Start by doing some historical research; find out as much about the site as you can. Do divination to see if going beyond that is a good idea. If you have good relations with the local nature spirits -- and you should have gotten to that stage at least before doing something with a prehistoric site! -- talk to them and see what they have to say about it. Then, if all the other indications are good, yes.

Now I think it may have been demonic as her behaviour became increasingly paranoid and aggressive. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Unfortunately yes. What kind of entity you attract is determined by your own spiritual state. As Marlowe pointed out in Doctor Faustus, summoning demons is the easiest thing in the world; all you have to do is descend to their level, and there they are. In the same way, if you want to interact with angels, you have to rise to their level -- which takes considerably more work. If the Neopagan you know set out to invoke a goddess (which is more or less what Neopagans consider the Sheela-na-gig to be) when her own mind was dominated by feelings such as rage, entitlement, and selfishness, yeah, she could quite easily end up with a demon.

replies.title: Re: Eye of Newt Not Available In Your Country

comments.title: Protection for a building

Hi JMG and commentariat!

Recently, a public little hut by a pond in a local park got burned down for the second time, and I saw that as an opportunity to ask the authorities wether I should build them a more fire-proof, prettier, and more ecologically sound replacement out of cob and reclaimed materials.

My motivation here was two-fold: First, I'd like to get a foot in the door as an artist-builder of such structures, because I think that lovely way of building needs to reach a larger audience, I'd like to make a living doing what I love, and what better way to clear away possible legal obstacles than to construct the demonstration building as a commission for the town?

The other reason is that I'm convinced the old building fell victim to arson because of its hideousness. It was dark, dirty, difficult to look into - in one word: scary. In fact, the prettiness of the place is opnly visible now that the thing is gone.

In keeping with its architectural flaws, it was only ever used by the local bums and drunkards, and though the arsonist hasn't been cought, it's a common local belief that some neighnor destroyed it to get rid of them.
Since I'm a firm believer in the power of aesthetics I'm confident that a harmonious, bright, inviting building will attract neither that audience (at least not as exclusively as its predecessor), nor that kind of violence.

Given the negativity of some bystanders now that I've started the build ("you're just wasting your time here, some idiot's gonna destroy that one, too!"), and the present air of hopelessness and desperation one can feel in so many small towns, I do wonder what techniques of additional protection besides my own optimism and good design I can implement, though.

Any suggestions are appreciated!

replies.title: (no subject)

Okay, you've got the entire subject by the wrong end. First of all, blowback has nothing to do with morality. The energies you invoke in magical workings will manifest in your life as well as in your target. It doesn't matter what your motives are, or what your target is, or any of that -- whatever you invoke, you attune yourself to, and the results follow. It's as impersonal and inescapable as the law of gravity.

Second, you don't win in magical combat by tearing down the other side -- you win by strengthening your own side. That's the lesson Dion Fortune taught in The Magical Battle of Britain. The Nazis were doing magic to harm their enemies; Fortune did magic to bring benefic forces into the British group mind, to strengthen and spiritualize it so that it wasn't vulnerable to Nazi sorceries. You doubtless know who won.

So don't fixate on those institutions. Do workings instead to bless and strengthen the alternatives, or to bless and strengthen the groupmind of the United States so that people aren't vulnerable the debased sorcery that's directed at them. That's the way to triumph in this kind of struggle.

replies.title: Re: New Age chakra balancing and Tibetan shamanism
replies.content:
I have a little experience with Tenzin Wangyal Rinpoche and the Bön tradition I can share!

In my opinion, he's absolutely the real deal: one of the wisest and kindest people I've had the pleasure of interacting with. His recent work is a gentle, approachable, and powerful distillation of Dzogchen for a western audience. It's been extremely helpful for me in terms of developing peace and spaciousness, seeing myself and the world more clearly, etc. In the terms we use here, it's generally more "mystical" than "magical".

His earlier work (like the book you mention) is much more of a traditional Tibetan presentation of pieces of Tibetan religion and magic. That stuff is also the real deal, but in my opinion it's not necessarily easy as a westerner to do it the way it should be done. Traditionally you'd be working closely with a guru, with a pretty intensive set of preliminary practices, having taken a set of hair-raising lifetime vows first, etc. I trust his wisdom in choosing to write his books, so I expect you probably can't mess yourself up too badly if you stick to the practices as written, but there are absolutely practices in the broader Bön tradition that need a great deal of care to get right, and even his books generally aren't pulling many punches.

Personally, I've gotten a lot out of the Bön practices I was taught, both in mundane senses (joy and energy) and less mundane senses (developing a little clairvoyance, etc.) But I quickly realized I was playing with forces I Did Not Understand, and that were Not Necessarily Safe. I've since put a pause on my work with Bön (except for a little bit of mantra recitation to fulfill a commitment I made), and am instead working through our host's Learning Ritual Magic, to give myself a firm basis of understanding and practice in something that's an easier cultural fit.

comments.title: Names and Egregors
comments.content:
Recent discussions on your blogs on topics such as affirmations and egregors have got me thinking about a potentially related magical topic: names. If I recall correctly, the Romans had a saying which translates into “As is a name, such is a fate” (if true, I’ve got quite a road ahead of me as my given name, in Old Norse, means “ruler of the gods” 😊). I am thinking here about names given to social constructs rather than individual human. Certainly, in some societies, names are taken very seriously. In Hindu India, business owners commonly include the name of their chosen god in their business name; and the Chinese are famous for including the word “luck” or other fortunate words in their business names.

One specific example has crossed my mind repeatedly: the Canadian alt-rock band named “The Tragically Hip”. The band achieved a level of popularity in my country which was unparalleled in living memory (so, it was definitely “hip”), but it ultimately experienced tragedy when its lead singer and lyricist, Gord Downie, died of inoperable brain cancer in 2017. comments.title: Working with multiple pantheons
comments.content: Good morning all! General question for whoever might want to weigh in...I was wondering what people's experience was when working with a specific practice, say SOP with Welsh deities, while maintaining a relationship with a deity from a completely different part of the globe-Anubis, for instance. Comments, suggestions, data points? Thanks as always to our kind host for being so generous with his time.

replies.title: Re: Working with multiple pantheons
replies.content: It happened constantly in ancient times. For example, the Gaulish goddess Epona ended up becoming the patron goddess of the imperial Roman cavalry, so that a cavalry unit stationed in Egypt, say, would make offerings to Epona as part of their ordinary regimental worship, and then go to the local temples to make offerings to Horus and Hathor, or whoever the local deities happened to be. Meanwhile in Britannia, the mysteries of Isis were celebrated by Roman Britons who worshiped Celtic gods the rest of the time. So you should be fine.

1) If it's sincere, it's good for the person who forgives, but each person makes their own karma and no one else can change it for them. Sorry.

It very often points to self-defeating behavior patterns. It can also indicate restriction, exile, isolation, and hidden enemies. replies.title: Re: Working With Dreams / Mugwort
replies.content:
Hi David,

Dreamwork was something I was into for quite awhile, and I still try to write down the big dreams. The teacher I got the most out of in terms of dreamwork was Robert Moss, who is also something of a shamanic practitioner, so I think you would really dig some of his books. When working with dreams I still use his approach.

I've used mugwort smudge in ritual as an aid for astral journeys. It's quite potent. What I don't like about it as something to use before bed for lucid dreaming is that it will induce very imaginal meanderings for a brief period but then you snap out of it and it is hard to go back to sleep.

Which brings me to a differentiation between lucid dreaming and active dreaming. For me my most powerful dreams have never been lucid and all came unbidden. In some I became aware I was dreaming in the process and that almost always brings me out of the dream. I don't think a person needs to control the dream (not recommended really). Rather in the approach Moss teaches you work with the dreams to bring their gifts into waking life. Sometimes you may have a lucid dream, just from working with them more, but not always.

Feel free to email me about dream stuff if you want.

& for all you creative writers out there: keeping a dream journal is great practice for writing fiction and a great secret laboratory of inspiration.

replies.title: Re: Putting a Jinx on a Litterbug

I really don't recommend it. When you work with negative energies -- like the energies in a curse -- those energies will invariably manifest in your own life as well. This is what I call the raspberry jam law of magic: with magic as with raspberry jam, you can't spread it on anything else without getting some on yourself.

Turn the question around and think of it in a different way. What is your will? Is it to hurt people? That's all that cursing does. Is it to get people to change their ways? That's less toxic but still problematic; if your magic seeks to control people that will make you more easily controlled by others. Is it to bless the nature trail? That's a suitable intention, because the energies of blessing will manifest in your life as well as in the trail.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
The name of Ostara sounds suspiciously similar to Ishtar and Astarte. Is she related to these Mesopotamian goddesses? If so, would this be a counter-evidence for the controversial "Proto-Indo-European" hypothesis?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm.  Ostara comes from a well-documented proto-Germanic root meaning "to shine," and it has plenty of cognates in Germanic languages; the English word "east," for example, meant "the bright place, the place where the lights comes from" originally. That wouldn't work if the name of the goddess was borrowed from Mesopotamia. On the other hand, the etymology of the name Ishtar apparently isn't well understood -- it's got cognates in various other Middle Eastern languages, but not the same flurry of related word Ostara has in Germanic languages. I suppose it's possible that the borrowing went the other way around; the Mitanni and the Hittites, who were neighbors of the Sumerians and Babylonians, spoke Indo-European languages, so it's just possible that a name cognate to Ostara ended up being borrowed by the Babylonians for the goddess the Sumerians called Inanna.

Whoever wrote the Orphic Hymns did so as part of participation in a full-blown mystery tradition; Dionysus and Persephone and death and resurrection and all the rest of it. So, I pulled out my copy of The Mysteries of Merlin and thumbed back to the appendix that talks about constructing one's own mystery tradition, and decided that it would be really nifty if there were a complete system of druid revival mysteries centered around Taliesin, complete with, yes, hymns to the deities.

Of course it occurred to me that this kind of thing should NOT BE RUSHED. It will take lots of meditation, divination to see if I should do it to begin with, lots of meditation, study of the Mabinogion and related myths, lots of meditation, careful examination of other mystery traditions and how they work, lots of meditation, much time spent praying and listening to and forming relationships with the deities in question, and some meditation wouldn't hurt either.

::feels strange burning sensation on thumb::

replies.title: Re: Hu Gadarn the memory god?

1) It sounds like a great idea.

2) The story of Taliesin has been used as a basis for Druid initiation rituals since the beginning of the Druid Revival; you'll want to read Lewis Spence's books on Druidry for some of this material, and the legend is also discussed in the forthcoming second volume of my book The Dolmen Arch.

replies.title: Re: Hu Gadarn the memory god?
replies.content: You wouldn't say that. To make something an adjective in Welsh you simply put it after the noun. "The Order of Taliesin," for example, would be Urdd Daliesin (the T becomes a D because Urdd is a feminine singular noun, and so the word following it undergoes what's called soft mutation).

comments.content:
John--

I have a question re an issue with which I've been dealing for some time, though I've only recently come to even a vague understanding of what it actually is (or might be).

For a long time (much of my lie to this point, actually), I've wrestled with thought-patterns, habits of thought, perspectives, and the like, which i can clearly see now to be unhelpful. Things like struggling to know, needing to know "why," feeling like I'm being confined to the smallness of my life b/c I'm somehow incapable or otherwise unworthy of greater things (whatever "greater things" might mean). Whiny in a Star Wars Prequel Anakin kind of way ("he's holding me back!"). Insisting that the wall in front of me shouldn't be there, but it is, and I'm kept from the thing on the other side that I want even though I know it's not what I think it is.

I've gotten to a point of awareness where, rather than blindly falling into these moods, I can see it happening--even if I'm not always able to pull myself out of it. It is a voice I hear quite clearly, an angry, bitter voice, demanding and unsatisfied.

I have received information from other sources which suggest that this voice is somehow associated with an ancestor of mine: specifically with a maternal great-great grandfather. Whether the voice is him or an energy/thought-form associated with him, I don't know, but the little of what I do know of him makes the notion plausible. This great-great grandfather was, according to family history, a civil servant of some kind back in Imperial Germany. We have a couple of old family photos from the 1880s and 1890s from my grandmother's estate. He remained in Germany, whereas his son (my great-grandfather) came to the US in 1922. From what we know, he died in Germany in the mid-1930s. He would have been someone serving the Kaiser's government who not only saw his nation lose the Great War, but then had to witness firsthand the aftermath and the chaos in the years following as the Weimar Republic stumbled about. I could see a man doomed to watching everything he knew fall apart around him.

The person (a friend of my wife's) who sensed this entity/energy involved with me described an angry, older man in some kind of uniform. Someone craving control of the events around him. When I brought up the picture of my great-great grandfather, she was certain that was who she'd seen.

My questions are:

1) What am I dealing with here? My ancestor or something associated with him or something else entirely?

2) What else can I do about it? (Daily SOP has been standard practice for several years now.)

I'd be grateful for any guidance you might offer.

--David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
David--

Oh ugh! That makes it harder to deal with, to the best of my fairly limited understanding of such things.

I had hoped you might only be dealing with what I think of as a wraith, that is, a fixed remnant of a person that has no sentience or awareness of its surroundings, but is just "going through the motions" endlessly, over and over, like a stuck record. It's not too hard to "unstick the record" in such a case.

So ... different magicians, different magics, and all that. What I would do with such an ancestor, if he were mine, might not be what our host would do with him, or what Dion Fortune, or Ernie Butler, or Chevalier Louis de B-- might do. That said, ...

First, I would set aside an extended quiet time in a place when and where I won't be interrupted. Before starting, I would remind myself firmly that if it comes down to an actual test of strength between the two of us, I have the advantage, for I am alive and he is not. This is not arrogance on my part, but a simple statement of fact.

I like to use a Psalm verse here, before starting, to reinforce my own sense of the power which the living always have against such beings: "What is man, that thou art mindful of him? and the son of man, that thou visitest him? For thou hast made him a little lower than God, and hast crowned him with glory and honor." (Yes, the King James reads "a little lower than the angels," and even the author of the Epistle to the Hebrews quotes the same verse in Greek with "angels." But the Hebrew is Elohim, and that otherwise usually means God.) -- BTW, there is power in these Psalms even if one is neither Christian or Jewish.

Once I had gone all still and silent, both inside and outside, I would look around with inner "sight" for my ancestor. He would be there somewhere, and I would find him. Once I found him, I would start by talking gently with him, trying to learn what he thinks he's accomplishing by seeking to rule me and live my life. He almost certainly has his reasons, and they probably seem good and sufficient to him. Even so, I would point out, my life is mine to live, not his. And so forth ...

And, most important of all, I would love him as deeply as I could while I did this and said these things, and I would display my love to his every sense: he is, after all, flesh of my flesh and spirit of my spirit; without him, I would not exist at all, and if I cannot love him despite everything he does and has done, I cannot love myself.

That might prove more than sufficient enough right there. Almost certainly (from what you say) your great-great-grandfather died lost and forlorn, feeling rejected by everyone and everything he valued--but convinced he was right, even so, and all his descendants were wholly wrong. Such a dead person can often be reached through love and compassion.

But not always. If that isn't enough, then I'd flex some "muscle" and point out that, since I'm alive and he isn't, I do have the upper hand over him, now and forever. By paying close attention, I can notice and reject every effort of his to rule my life. I can ignore all his advice; I can reject his every attempt to program my mind and body; and so forth. And I intend to do just that, starting RIGHT NOW!

And then I'd start doing it, right that very minute. Surprisingly, it will be much easier to do so, now that I've served notice on him.

That might be enough to get rid of his influence over my life. But if he's really determined to win at any cost, though, he can try to terrify me into submission. Here my resource is simply not to allow myself to fear, not to be terrified, no matter what form his wrath may appear to take. ("... fear is the little death ...")

Or he may try to control me through guilt, or through shame. No matter; I won't allow myself to feel guilt or to feel shame at his behest. (One can construct a Litany against Guilt and a Litany against Shame modeled on the well-known Litany against Fear from "Dune.")

And finally, if he still persists with the fear and terror (or the guilt or the shame), even though it isn't working on me as it used to, I would "rise up" in full righteous rage, "yell" at him to "Get the [undruidly word] out of my life and stay out, or I'll put an end to you." Here is where I could visualize taking up the weapon that seemed most likely to terrify him--a bayonette, a sword, a cannon, a flame-thrower, whatever--and prepare to use it against him with lethal determination. He will either flee in terror, or I will use the weapon--all this is done within the quietness and stillness I mentioned at the start--against him and kill him.

There can legitimately be limits to the love one feels for anyone, ancestor or not, I would say. If he breaches those limits, he is done for.

Here, of course, is where that Psalm verse comes into its own: as a being "a little lower than God" I--and you--actually have the inborn power to do such a thing.

And then I would decisively exit the realm of stillness and silence within which I have been doing all these things. And go do something to mark my exiting: a spot of hard physical labor, a bath, some food--even a bacon cheeseburger! 😀

I should add ... This will be really hard work! You will, I reckon, feel like you've been put through the wringer as never before once you've finished it. And you will have been put through it!

But you are alive, and you will recover. -- Besh wishes!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG (obviously), so take this for what it's worth, but my own experience has been that there isn't really anything we can offer them in the sense that we offer gifts to each other to help each other fulfill our needs. When I make offerings, it's with the same sense that I might write a poem or sing a song; it's a way of expressing what I feel towards the deity. I understand some deities to prefer one kind of offering over another in the same sense that it wouldn't be appropriate to accompany some bridal processions with the "Ride of the Valkyries", but for others it would be perfect.

But I do sometimes get a sense from one deity in particular that I can be, um, "useful" to her, in the same way that my very small kids are "useful" to me when I have yard work to do. Their "help" makes it take three times as long, but it's more important to me that they have a chance to successfully tackle challenges (with me there to see that they pick sensible challenges and succeed) and thereby grow. That might be particular to our relationship or not, I certainly don't know...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's a lot of confusion about offerings. In one sense, we can offer nothing to the gods -- everything is theirs anyway, and there is no lack in their lives that we can fill. In another sense, we offer them our love and respect, and signal that love and respect with offerings of incense or food or what have you. In a third sense, we offer them things, they bless those things, and we then consume them -- breathing in the scent of the incense, sharing out the food offering, or what have you -- and attune ourselves to them, so they can act through us in the human world.

As for offering up suffering, that's subtle. You don't get rid of the suffering by offering it to a deity -- you still feel it just as much as ever. What happens is that you allow the suffering to become the deity's own suffering. It's not that you're asking the deity to share your burden -- you're offering to share a little of the deity's burden. Thus the Christian who offers up her suffering to Christ is shouldering a tiny bit of the weight of the Cross, and so freeing up a little bit of grace that can be used to bless someone else. The same is true of any other unwelcome experience. When you offer up joy, on the other hand, you're sharing it with the deity, adding a tiny bit to the bliss of the Divine.

comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
Hello Arch Druid,

I am very much a novice when it comes to occulutism. Nevertheless, I have been thinking a lot about reincarnation lately and there is something about it I do not understand. I always thought the idea behind reincarnation was that those who lived enlightened lives would ascend to higher levels of being. They would move past lower life forms and eventually surpass humanity as well, eventually becoming greater spiritual beings. But if this is true, why are some of the spiritual beings that surpass us evil or at least comparable to humanity in our selfish, capricious ways? If I have to live many increasingly noble lives to become an angel or a god, why are there evil angels/gods? Is advancement based on something other than moral development? Could I be reincarnated as a drastically superior being after living my life as a consistent, evil genius? Do higher spiritual beings sometimes go bad (the fall of Lucifer comes to mind here)? Any clarity you could add would be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: Okay, first of all, you have to remember that myths and legends are written by human beings, not by gods. They portray the gods with human passions because that makes for a better and more comprehensible story! Occult philosophy has always taught that the myths shouldn't be taken literally, that their meaning is symbolic and often hidden behind various veils. (This is also the occult take on the Bible, by the way.) Those beings that have risen above the human level aren't perfect, but they're much wiser and better than we are, and what they are now, we will be in due time. Thus there are no evil gods. There are demons, but they're much more limited than we are -- notice how often they're associated with one and only one set of dysfunctional behaviors! -- and their power over us consists almost entirely in their ability to fool us into giving up our own power to them.

comments.title: Enlivening Deity Images
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,

A research topic that greatly interests me is the magical process of enlivening statues or sacred images of deities. That is, the invocation of a 'real presence' of the deity within the substance of the image, so that it becomes divinized - more than a representation. I believe that rebuilding the body of knowledge of how human beings can help bring holy places or sacred places into being is important, and such theurgical image-enlivening might be a valuable part of that knowledge. Of course, I would only experiment practically at all in this area if and when such seems appropriate, after suitable prayer, meditation, and divination.
So far, I've come up with the following lines of investigation:
I. Egypt. The enlivening and consecration process for Ancient Egyptians was the 'Opening of the Mouth' ritual, with variants for God images, coffins, and mummies - the version for mummies is especially well-documented.
II. Mesopotamia. There are two ceremonies for which texts survive, one also called the 'Opening of the Mouth' ritual, and another called the 'Washing of the Mouth' ritual.
III. India. The Agamas and Tantras are Hindu liturgical manuals describing mantras and other means to enliven or consecrate the image (which is called a murti), and there are many variants, including esoteric Tantric ones, of a Prana Pratishtha or 'establishing the prana' ritual in which 'the eye of the murti is opened.' There are also oral traditions.
IV. Tibet. An elaborate process involving mantras, Tantric visualizations, filling subtle energetic points of the statue with texts, relics, etc.
Much of this makes me wonder if a properly enlivened image may have a robust etheric presence.
I would much appreciate it if you could provide any sources or leads from the Western occult tradition, or suggest any additional sources to research. The only Western occult source I have found so far is contemporary (Josephine McCarthy's material - she outlines a couple of techniques), so I wonder - are you aware of anything that could be useful from the older sources and traditions? Overall, do you have any thoughts about the soundness of such a research project, or any suggestions about it?

Thank you very much for your time and for hosting these.
Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Enlivening Deity Images
replies.content: Well, have you read the discussion in the Asclepius, one of the essays in the Corpus Hermeticum? That references the Egyptian practice, but from a Greek sensibility. There are also recent scholarly works on ancient Greek statue magic, but I can't find the titles off hand. More broadly, though, I think this is an excellent project, and would be useful both to occultists and to polytheists.

3) I wouldn't dismiss out of hand the possibility that you're remembering an actual life. We know very, very little about classic Taoist internal alchemy, and I wouldn't put it past the realm of possibility that something of the sort could happen.

comments.title: Precognition?
comments.content:
Hullo JMG, I would very much like to get your take on a psychic experience I had this past weekend. I'll try to be as brief as possible:

It was Saturday morning, and I was listening to a paranormal podcast in which a gentleman recounted a personal experience he had involving precognition. Shortly thereafter, I took a midday nap (I was strangely pooped), and I had a dream in which I was traveling along, trying to divine things from the environment. I soon saw a tall tree which looked just like the ogham few Quert. I was tickled at that recognition, which I called out, but was unsure of what it really meant.

I awoke from the dream, and later performed my daily ogham divination. Partly to my surprise -- and partly to my expectation -- the third, "present" few that I drew was none other than Quert, in the exact upright and upwards facing position in which it appeared in my dream (cue the Twilight Zone music). According to my calculations, I figure the mathematical probability of that happening to be roughly 1 in 300, which is quite the mere "coincidence."

This isn't the first time I've had precognitive dreams (which apparently run in the family), but the unique thing about this particular instance is that it was immediately preceded by the aforementioned gentleman's account of precognition. In other words, it seems as though a seed was planted in my mind -- a desire to have such an experience -- and I subconsciously caused it to happen in my dream, with the effect manifesting in waking life. It seems as though I WILLED it to happen, internally, whereas before it was always external influences acting upon my consciousness.

My questions are:

1. Is this a common development for practitioners of magic? A byproduct of becoming more sensitive to higher planes? Or is it a more individual ability?

2. Are there specific exercises one can do to develop this specific ability and use it more consciously? Or does it naturally develop with daily practice and experience?

3. Or is it all just a giant fluke, and I have no real control over when it happens?

replies.title: Re: Precognition?
replies.content:
1) I think it's more of an individual ability. Most mages learn to get a sense of the future before it happens, but the sense comes in different ways. With me, it's a matter of intuitive "hunches" rather than dreams or visions.

2) I'm sure there are but I don't happen to know of any. Regular practice of divination seems to help, but it's a more general thing.

3) That I can't tell you.

replies.title: Re: Precognition?
replies.content: Alrighty then, thanks so much for the feedback, sir!

replies.title: Re: Precognition?
replies.content: Intuitive hunches are how this ability works with me, too.  Rarely they seem to have ancestral prompting to them; more often they don't, but just come out of the blue.  They don't always pan out, either.

I want to thank you for pointing me in the direction of studies on Proto-Indo-European deities.

Tinkering.

At any rate, your encouragement through your essays to tinker and adapt and keep adapting until something starts to settle has created my ideosyncratic ritual which seems to be working.

Bruce
